A railway has been formed, connecting with the Nile the quarries of the Mokattam mountains, behind Cairo (out of which the stones of the pyramids were hewn), in order to furnish stone for the work; and arrangements made for a vast supply of forest timber from the woods in the neighbourhood of Scanderoun. When the construction of the barrage was first determined on, the work was pursued with the greatest possible diligence: 34,000 purses (equal to more than £170,000 sterling) was expended, and very little security obtained for its proper application. The recklessness with which the first outlay was made, has, there is reason to believe, created some disgust in the mind of the viceroy, and prevented the execution of the work. Instead of gradually supplying materials wanted for the progress of the constructions, the whole mass was brought together, encumbering the adjacent ground, and embarrassing the workmen at every step. Nineteentwentieths of the labourers were removed, and the arrangements proceed sluggishly, without that unity of purpose and vigour of execution which presided at the first conception." [Bowring's Report.] It would certainly be a circumstance very much to be deplored, were the barrage of the Nile to be abandoned, now that it has so far progressed, and that so high a class of engineering skill and ability has been displayed in bringing the work to its present condition. The site of the barrage is about 5 French leagues to the N of Cairo, at the place where the river branches off to Rosetta on the one side, and to Damietta on the other. The apex of the D. constitutes the head of the dam; and a canal is constructed for the purpose of irrigating the prov. of Menufih, which, in point of fact, is the terra firma of the D. Over each of the two branches which flank the prov. of Menufih there are constructed solid bridges, the foundations of which are concrete, laid upon piles of timber driven into the bed of the river. These bridges, running from the apex of the D. to each of the opposite banks, constitute the barrage. They have a moving centre-piece, which turns upon itself, for the purpose of allowing boats, steamers, or other craft to go up or down the river; the arches of the bridges, 83 in number, are fitted with sluices which are movable, as at the sluices on the Thames at Moulsey, where if the water is low, as much as is needed to keep the lock full can always be dammed in; and should the river be full, free egress is given to the flood by removing the sluice-boards. In this manner will the DEL bridges or dams two canals are to be cut, for the purpose of conveying the water thus prevented from flowing in its present channels into the provinces of Sharkieh and Bahari, which they are destined to fertilize. The great difficulty which the engineer has had to overcome is the alluvial nature of the soil on which these stupendous works are based. The depth to which the alluvium of the river extends has been found so great as to baffle the attempt to penetrate beyond it, and to obtain a foundation for the piles of a more solid and staple nature. But it has been considered that the weight of the works themselves, and the solid character which has been carefully given to them, will prevent any settlement or sapping of their foundations, and the pressure of the water against them can always be lessened by opening the sluices, and by drawing the water off through the medium of the three canals of irrigation which will exist for the purposes of the D. and the provinces of Sharkieh and Bahari. The estimate furnished by Mougel Bey of the cost of the whole work amounted to 15,000,000 francs, or £600,000. The length of the bridge over the Damietta branch will be 543 French metres or 1,780 ft., with 45 arches; the Rosetta branch will be spanned by a bridge of 474 metres or 1,555 ft. in length, with 39 arches. The canal of the D. will be 100 metres = 328 ft. in width; that of the province of Sharkieh will have the same width; whilst the Bahari canal will only be 60 metres = 196 ft. wide, both being from 7 to 8 French leagues in length. It is calculated that they will irrigate an extent of land to the annual value of £5,000,000 in rental alone. The cubic measurement of concrete employed in making the foundations is 160,000 cubic metres; the masonry is 250,000 cubic metres; and the number of piles driven into the soil is 35,001, each measuring from 5 to 12 metres in length. Concrete will be used for all the submarine works, and the rest will be done with squared stone, rubble, and bricks. These materials are found in abundance in Egypt, and even in the immediate neighbourhood of the works. Artificial pozzalano is in general use, being obtainable in all parts of the country from pulverized bricks. This matter, which is analogous to that produced by volcanoes, being mixed with lime and rubble, forms what is called beton or concrete. Before the discovery of this pozzalano, which costs 5 francs the cubic metre on the spot, it was formerly brought from Italy, at the expense of 45 f. or 50 f. the cubic metre. The cost of the bridge has been estimated at 7,000,000 f., and cannot exceed 10,000,000 f. When once the materials are collected, it will require no more than three years for 5,000 men to complete this colossal undertaking, which will vie in grandeur with the celebrated monuments of ancient Egypt. For the divisions and topography of the D. the reader is referred to the general article on EGYPT.
DELTA, a village of Lee township, Oneida co., in the state of New York, U. S., 112 m. NW of Albany. Pop. in 1840, 350.
DELTING, a parish of Shetland, on the N coast of the mainland. Pop. 2,019.
DELVIN, a rivulet of Leinster, which runs along the confines of Dublin and Meath counties, and falls into the Irish sea, 1 m. NNW of Balbriggan.

DELVINAKI, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Albania, in the sanjak and 64 m. SE of Valona, and 25 m. WNW of Janina, seated in a large circular hollow, around which nothing but bleak and barren rocks appear. It contained 4 churches and 350 houses in 1813; but in 1834 was described as consisting of a small group of cottages, having probably been reduced to this condition by Ali Pasha's extortions.

DELVINO, or Delonia, a livas, or inferior pashalic, and town of Turkey in Europe, in Albania.—The livas is bounded on the N and NE by the sanjak of Valona, on the E by that of Janina, and on the S and SW by the Ionian sea, along which it extends from the Khimara mountains on the NW, to the river Acheron on the SE, a distance of about 70 m. Its average breadth is nearly 25 m. It is generally mountainous. Besides the lofty ridges which run along the confines, there are the parallel ranges of Serueles in the N, of Philates and Pharmaco near the centre of the sanjak, and of Vlachoritico towards the S. The two latter ranges enclose the basin of the Kalamas-the only river of importance which is contained in this portion of Albania, and which discharges itself into the Ionian sea, to the N of the gulf of Gomenizza. The Pavla, on an affluent of which the cap. is situated, and the Bistritza, fall into Lake Viveri. The minor streams are mere mountain-torrents, which generally become dry in summer. Olives and olive-oil form the chief productions waters of the Nile be pent or dammed up, whilst above the two of the soil. Towards the E, wood and pasture-land cover the greater extent of the surface; the latter is employed in the feeding of cattle. Fish is abundant on the coasts. The pop. of D. consists chiefly of Greeks and Arnauts.—The town of D. is situated on the slope of the Khimara range, near an affluent of the Pavla, 10 m. NE of the entrance of the latter river into Lake Viveri or Butrinto, 60 m. ENE of Janina, and 16 m. from the NE extremity of the island of Corfu. It is strongly fortified, and contains several mosques. Its trade consists chiefly in olive oil, cattle, and other agricultural productions. The pop. is variously stated. Pouqueville estimated it to contain 600 houses in 1806.

DEMAK, or Damak, a town of Java, cap. of a prov. of the same name, on the N coast of the island, 15 m. NE of Samarang, situated on the l. bank of a river of the same name, which, after a NW course of about 24 m., throws itself into the sea of Java. D. was formerly the capital of a principality, and is still of considerable extent, but its pop. does not now heide. exceed 3,000. The surrounding country is level, consisting of an extensive marsh, and a plain of great fertility, which is intersected by numerous canals and watercourses.
DEMAND, a township of Randolph co., in the state of Arkansas, U.S. Pop. in 1840, 319.
DEMANGE-AUX-EAUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Meuse, cant. of Gondrecourt, 15 m. from Commercy. Pop. 772. It has several blastfurnaces and forges.
DEMARCATION (POINT), a headland on the Arctic coast of America, in 141° W long., marking the boundary between the British and Russian dominions in that quarter.

DEMAS (Ras), a low neck of land on the E coast of Tunis, to the N of Cape Mahadia, in N lat. 35° 38', E long. 11° 5'. On this cape is a village of the same name, which occupies the site of the ancient Tapsus, the ruins of which are still to be traced.

DEMA'VEND, a mountain of Persia, in the prov. of Mazanderan, in N lat. 35° 50', E long. 52° 12', 42 m. ENE of Tehran. It is reputed to be the highest summit in the Elburz range. According to M. Tresel, a French traveller, who crossed the Mazanderan range at two different points, the alt. of D. above the level of the city of Tehran is 8,533 ft. As the same gentleman found Tehran to be 3,200 French, or 3,412 English ft. above the Caspian, it would follow that the absolute height of D. is 11,946 ft. above that sea. But if Fraser's estimate of 3,666 ft. as the height of Tehran be admitted, and it be also admitted that the Caspian sea is 332 ft. below the level of other seas, then the elevation of D. will be 12,531 above that sea, or 12,200 above the level of the Persian gulf. It is difficult, however, to conceive that, if D. be only 8,533 ft. above the level of Tehran, it can be distinctly visible at a direct distance of 150 m., or 180 m. according to Colonel Johnston. Humboldt calculates that it would require an elevation of 13,126 ft. to be barely visible at such a distance. We must therefore conclude, either that the elevation of D. is much greater than it is made by the observation of Tresel, or attribute its distant visibility to the effect of refraction; which is sometimes so extraordinarily great in the upland plains of Persia, that an object, though 12 m. distant, seems only 2 m. to the astonished traveller. The admeasurements of Mr. Ainsworth, founded on Mr. Thomson's barom. observations, and approaching, Mr. Ainsworth thinks, to within 100 ft. of the truth, give 4,000 ft. for the elevation of Tehran; 5,800 ft. for that of the v. of Ask; 6,700 ft. for Germah; and 14,700 ft. for that of the peak. It is a volcanic mountain. According to Fraser's information, its summit contains a large hollow in its centre, as if a mine had been wrought | m. N of Ousman. DEM into it, with several small excavations in different directions. There can be little doubt that these are extinguished craters: the summit being formed chiefly of pumice and scoriæ, with debris of basalt, and the whole mountain abounding in sulphur. Mr. W. T. Thomson, in Sept. 1837, succeeded in gaining the summit of D., which he found to be a cone composed of soft rock for about 100 ft. from the summit, and filled with pure sulphur, which is easily dug out of the crevices with a piece of stick. Very severe earthquakes are sometimes felt at a v. which lies 23 m. S of it, and the volcanic action is visible in the contorted and twisted strata of rocks throughout the surrounding country. At the v. of Germah, on the S side of the mountain, are hot springs of a temp. of 148°, and at Ask such springs are very numerous.

DEMBAKANI, or DENBAKANE, a town of Senegambia, in the kingdom of Futatoro, near the l. bank and on an affluent of the Senegal, 90 m. SSE of Ka- DEMBARARI, DAMBARARI, or DARAMBARI, a town of Monomotapa, in the district of Moveranga, 90 m. SE of Chicova, and 180 m. W of Sene. It was formerly large and flourishing, and had an important gold market; but is now in ruins, and deserted by its inhabitants. DEMBEA, TSANA, or ZANA,—the SENA-BARSENA or La-Mar-de-Sena of the Portuguese missionaries, -a lake of Abyssinia, occupying the lowest part of the depression of the table-land of Amhara. Its surface has an alt. of 6,110 ft. above sea-level. Its length from N to S is about 60 m.; its average breadth 25 m. It is intersected by the parallel of 12° N, and the meridian of 37° 12' E. The Bahr-el- Azrek, or Blue Nile, enters it on the SW side, and reissues from its SE side. According to Bruce's map, it receives a prodigious number of small streams, chiefly on its E side. Ludolf says there are 11 islands in it. It has the prov. of D. on its N side; that of Biegemider on the E; and Miecha on the S and W. Bruce says that all the surrounding country seems once to have been occupied by the lake, whose waters are "visibly upon the decrease."
DE'MBECHA, a large town of Abyssinia, in the prov. of Wustagult, 27 m. SSE of Gudera. It contains a celebrated monastery.

DEMBIA, a river on the Upper Guinea coast of Africa, 20 m. from the Ponga, between which and the D. a low mangrove shore intervenes. It flows into the Atlantic, in N lat. 9° 50', to the N of the estuary of the Debrika.
DEMBICA, a town of Galicia, in the gov. of Lemberg, circle and 20 m. ENE of Tarnow, on the r. bank of the Wysloka.
DEMBLEBY, a parish of Lincolnshire, 4 m. NW of Folkingham. Area 1,000 acres. Pop. 58.

DEMBOS, a province of Lower Guinea, in the eastern part of the kingdom of Angola. It is described by Douville-whose whole narrative, however, must be taken with distrust—as situated between 50 and 80 m. to the N of the Coanza, on the continuation of a chain of hills, which, crossing that river about 130 m. above the sea, runs N, or NE, and seems to form the margin of an elevated terrace or table-land towards the E.
DEMBOWIEC, a town of Galicia, in the gov. of Lemberg, circle and 4 m. S of Jaslo, on the l. bank of the Dembowca. Pop. 1,590. Linen and crape form the chief articles of local manufacture.
DEMBRA, a village of Anatolia, in the sanjak of Meis, near the head of the bay of Andraki. It seems to mark the site of the ancient Myra.
DEMCHINSK, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 96 m. SW of Tambov, in the district and 8

DEMEN, or DEHMEN, a village of Mecklenburg- Schwerin, in the bail. and 6 m. NE of Krivitz. Pop. 652. It has an annual fair. DEMENYFALWA, DEMENFALVA, or DEMANOwa, a village of Hungary, in the comitat of Liptau, 8 m. E of Lipese, and 35 m. N of Brezno. It is noted for the ice-grotto of Drachenhoehle, which occurs in the side of a limestone mountain in the environs; and of which Mr. Paget has given a description in the 1st vol. of his work on Hungary.

DEMER, a river of Belgium, which takes its rise at S. Heeren-Elderen, near Tongres, in the prov. of Limburg; makes a sweep to the NW; passes under the walls of Hasselt; thence directs its course to the W; enters the prov. of S. Brabant; passes Diest and Aerschot; and falls into the Dyle at Werchter, 6 m. below Louvain. It runs a total distance of 54 m., of which 21 are navigable. Its principal affluents are the Herk, Gette, and Velpe, all on the l. side.

DEMERARA, a county of British Guayana, generally described, and united in its statistical details, with the adjacent county of Essequibo. The tract comprehended in this united settlement is of much larger extent than that of Berbice. It extends from the Abari creek, by which it is divided from Berbice, to the Coyuni river, which flows into the estuary of the Orinoco, -a space of above 250 m. of direct distance along the coast. Its interior boundaries are not so easily determined. From the sources of the Demerara river to those of the Mazaruni, or great western branch of the Essequibo, the interior extent from E to W is 360 m. The extent, however, is still larger if the whole course of the river Essequibo be included, which, in a direct line, is at least 420 m. from SE to NW; or, if a line be drawn from the supposed source of the Essequibo, in the S angle of the Tumucurag mountains, to those of the Mazaruni, in the angle formed by the junction of the mountains of Usupama and Itamac, the length will exceed 630 m. from SE to NW. The medial breadth may be estimated at 150 m. from S to N, or from the sea to the mountains. Physical divisions.] The whole of the tract possessed, or in future to be possessed, by this united colony, may be divided into five regions, viz.: the coast; the lower courses of the Essequibo and the D. rivers; the valley watered by the Mazaruni; the valley of the Cuyuwini; and that watered by the Upper Essequibo. The coast only, and the lower courses of the Essequibo and the D., have been brought under cultivation, and as yet but partially. The large and probably fertile valley of the Mazaruni is separated on the W from the valley of the Caroni, in Venezuela, by the mountains of Usupama; and that of the Cuyuwini on the N, by the mountains of Itamac or Imataca, from the valley and delta of the Orinoco. An immense tract of excellent land lies along and betwixt these two rivers. The basin of the Essequibo is of vast dimensions, being separated from the valley of the Parima, in Venezuela, by the mountains of Kauritta on the W; and by the Sierra Acarai from Portuguese Guayana on the S. In the general article Guayana these boundaries are more particularly noticed. Rivers.] The chief rivers of this united colony are the Essequibo and the D. The former is the largest of all the streams that descend from the mountains of Guayana into the Atlantic, between the mouths of the Maranon and those of the Orinoco. It is composed of four main branches, the Essequibo from the Sierra Acarai; the Rupununi, a river of about 220 m. in length; the Mazaruni; and the Cuyuni. See Essequibo.—The D., though a large stream, is but a streamlet compared to the Essequibo: its course from SE to NW, not exceeding 85 m. in direct distance from the sea to where it ceases to be navigable. Beyond the falls, in N lat. 5° 19', it branches out into two streams, one coming from the SW, and another from the SE; but the exact source of these two branches is unknown, the river not having been explored beyond its extreme point. Running parallel to the Essequibo on the W, and the Berbice on the E, it does not receive any tributary of magnitude. At its entrance, the D. river is 11 m. broad, and sheltered from every wind; it is never visited by those tremendous hurricanes so frequent in tropical climates, and so destructive in the West India islands, and it here forms one of the finest harbours in the world. Unfortunately, however, a bar of sand stretches across its mouth, over which no vessel drawing more than 9 ft. water can pass until halfflood, at high water in spring-tides, when the bar is covered to the depth of 18 ft., but still requires very cautious navigation. The river is muddy, and very brackish in the lower part of its course; but from the sand-hills to the falls it is fresh, and its water very clear. Its average current is about 21 knots. Climate.] The mean temp. of D. for the year is 81°.2; the maximum 90°; the minimum 74°. The dry season commences on the coast towards the end of August, and continues to the end of November; after which showers follow to the end of January. The dry season commences in the middle of February. and continues to the middle of April, when the rains begin to descend in torrents, and the inundations of the rivers commence. The months of October and November are the finest in the year. The temp. of the interior is still more mild, and the climate is healthy and particularly free from alternations of heat and cold. The range of mortality is only about 1 in 37 to 40, even of the labouring pop. This salubrity may be attributed in part to the rapid drainage, and partly to the purity of the air. The prevailing diseases are dysenteries, diarrhœa, and fevers. The bilious remittent or jungle fever sometimes assumes here the more permanent and active type of yellow fever. The only great inconveniences attending a residence in D. are the want of good water, and the vast myriads of insects with which man has to contend in these regions. The river water is brackish for many miles from the coast; there are no springs, and wells would only supply a water that could not be used. Though the canals bring fresh water in great plenty from the woods, yet it is so strongly impregnated with infused leaves, and other vegetable matter, as to be both very unpleasant to the eye and the palate. The only remedy for this evil is that of preserving the rain-water; and immense tanks or cisterns have been constructed for this purpose on almost every estate. The rain-water is sometimes preserved by families in large earthen jars, in which, with much care, it may be kept good during the whole of the dry season. Those who are not possessed of tanks or jars must either content themselves with the strongly macerated infusion of the forests, called 'bush-water,' or send parties many miles with boats and casks for fresh water from the distant streamlets that descend to the rivers. Yet the climate of D. is distinguished for its extreme moisture. in 1831, no less than 157 inches of rain fell in Georgetown, or nearly 6 times the quantity which falls annually in Great Britain. In fact, the rain sometimes comes down like a cataract, and so profusely as to fill every vat and cistern in the colony; but then it does not fall for many months together; and a period of 18 months has sometimes passed over with only a light shower or two. Soil, &c.] From the immense extent of the interior tracts watered by noble streams, it is obvious that a very small portion of British Guayana is yet subjected to the labours of the industrious planter; and that an important field of future agricultural and colonial industry is here opened to view. At no very distant epoch, British Guayana may eventually become of much greater consequence than all the British West India islands put together. The soil is superior to that of the West Indies; and in many of the latter islands we know that the soil, once so fertile and productive, is now barren and unproductive, being completely worn out. If we add to this, that no hurricanes visit this favoured coast, and that consequently the planters, in addition to having to work upon greater fertility of soil, are not subjected to sudden losses in shipping, buildings, and produce, it need not occasion surprise that these colonies are fast rising into importance. The depth of the soil is generally 12 ft. on the coast; and it is so prolific as in some places to produce 30 successive crops of rice without exhaustion. The numerous canals by which the flat country on the coast is alternately drained, being annually scoured, the mud brought down by the waters from the woods, and thus removed, forms a coating of manure, and equally serves to raise and enrich the soil: the soil is thus as it were continually renewed, and is never suffered to wear out. Animal kingdom.] The animal kingdom here presents the Jaguar or S. American tiger, the puma or American lion, and several others of the more formidable carnivora. Numerous birds and varieties of monkeys people the forests; and the anteater, the sloth, the armadillo, and the opossum, are also seen there. Among the animals which serve as food to man, are the maypuri or tapir, the capibara, the labba, the agouti, the caruni or wild hog, the peccari or Mexican hog, and different species of deer.—The ornithology of D. is peculiarly rich and diversified. The muddy banks of the rivers flame with the scarlet curlew. At sunset the pelicans return from the sea to the courada trees. The columbine, gallinaceous, and passerine tribes, people the fruit-trees; and the splendid humming-birds glance to and fro among the flowers. At the close of day, the vampires, or winged bats, suck the blood of the traveller, and cool him by the flap of their wings. "He whose eye can distinguish the various beauties of uncultivated nature, and whose ear is not shut to the wild sounds in the woods, will," says the enthusiastic Waterton, " be delighted in passing up the river D. Every now and then, the maam or tinamou sends forth one long and plaintive whistle from the depth of the forest, and then stops; whilst the yelping of the toucan, and the shrill voice of the bird called pi-pi-yo, is heard during the interval. The campanero never fails to attract the attention of the passenger; at a distance of nearly 3 m. you may hear this snow-white bird tolling every four or five minutes, like the distant convent-bell. From 6 to 9 in the morning the forests resound with the mingled cries and strains of the feathered race; after this, they gradually die away. From 11 to 3 all nature is hushed as in a midnight silence, and scarce a note is heard, saving that of the campanero and the pi-pi-yo; it is then that, oppressed by the solar heat, the birds retire to the thickest shade, and wait for the refreshing cool of evening. At sun-down the vampires, bats, and goat-suckers, dart from their lonely retreat, and skim along the trees on the river's bank. The different kinds of frogs almost stun the ear with their hoarse and hollow sounding croaking; while the owls and goat-suckers lament and mourn all night long. About two hours before day-break, you will hear the red monkey moaning as though in deep distress; the houtou, a solitary bird, and only found in the thickest recesses of the forest, distinctly articulates 'houtou! houtou!' in a low and plaintive tone, an hour before sun-rise; the maam whistles about the same hour; the hannaquoi, pataca, and maroudi, announce his near approach to the eastern horizon, and the parrots and paroquets confirm his arrival there."-The insect-tribes are oppressively numerous in D. "In truth," says Pinckard, "the general buzzing, the biting, stinging, creeping, and crawling of these tormenting objects, distress me far more than the heat, or any apprehension of disease. We are bitten, stung, or over-run by day and by night, and exposed to incessant pain and discomfort, unless constantly upon the watch, or carefully protected by some defensive covering, being perpetually beset with myriads of flies, ants, musketoes, cockroaches, lizards, Jack-Spaniards-a large species of wasp-fireflies, centipedes, &c., which, in addition to their bites and stings, fly in our faces, crawl about our persons, and make an intolerable buzzing in our ears. In an evening, and particularly after rain, the confused noise of these humming hosts is peculiarly disagreeable. It conveys the idea of breathing in an atmosphere of sounds, or amidst one vast animated hive, where every created insect joins in full chorus; the enormous frog of the country croaking the bass in a voice resembling in some degree the bellowing of an ox." After such testimony, the reflections of an Edinburgh reviewer on the subject of D. entomology, will not appear exaggerated, and are too lively to be here omitted. "Insects are the curse of tropical climates. The bête rouge lays the foundation of a tremendous ulcer. In a moment you are covered with ticks. Chigoes bury themselves in your flesh, and hatch a large colony of young chigoes in a few hours. They will not live together, but every chigoe sets up a separate ulcer, and has its own private portion of pus. Flies get entry into your mouth, into your eyes, into your nose; you eat flies, drink flies, and breathe flies. Lizards, cockroaches, and snakes, get into the bed; ants eat up the books; scorpions sting you on the foot. Every thing bites, stings, or bruises; every second of your existence you are wounded by some piece of animal life that nobody has ever seen before, except Swammerdam and Meriam. An insect with eleven legs is swimming in your teacup, a nondescript with nine wings is struggling in the small beer, or a caterpillar with several dozen eyes in his belly is hastening over the bread and butter! All nature is alive, and seems to be gathering all her entomological hosts to eat you up, as you are standing, out of your coat, waistcoat, and breeches. Such are tropics. All this reconciles us to our dews, fogs, vapours, and drizzle-to our apothecaries rushing about with gargles and tinctures-to our old, British, constitutional cough, sore throats, and swelled faces."-Of the saurian tribe, the cayman and the alligator occur here: the former in the interior, the latter on the coasts. The pipa, or Guayana frog, hideous as a toad, and of the size of a duck, abounds in all the pools. Land tortoises and fresh-water turtle are abundant. The camudi snake has been killed from 30 to 40 ft. long; he does not act by venom, but by size and convolution. The Spaniards affirm that he grows to the length of 80 ft., and that he will swallow a bull. There is a whipsnake of a beautiful green, and the labarri snake of a dirty brown, both peculiarly dangerous. Every lovely colour under heaven is lavished upon the counachouchi, the most venomous of reptiles, and known by the name of 'the bush-master.' Man and beast, says Mr. Waterton, fly before him, and allow him to pursue an undisputed path.—The rivers of the interior teem with delicious fish in great variety. The arapaima, or pirarucu, one of the largest fresh-water fishes, attains occasionally a length of 12 ft. in the Rupununi, and weighs upwards of 300 lbs. It is used both fresh and salted by the Indians. The laulau reaches nearly an equal size. The pacu, found near the rapids of the Essequibo, is a flat fish, 20 inches in length, and weighing 4 lbs. Commercial productions.] Sugar, rum, molasses, and coffee, are the staple articles of produce in D.; but the soil is well-adapted to the cultivation of cotton, coffee, cocoa, the vine, and the olive. Of the cereal grains, Indian corn or maize is indigenous, and is most successfully cultivated by the Indians in the interior. Rice forms the principal article of food for the labouring pop.; and there is a tract between the rivers Berbice and Essequibo, in N lat. 4° 20', admirably adapted to its culture. The productiveness of the soil is so great that the Indian bestows but little labour on the cultivation of his provisionfield: he plants cassada, maize, plantains, sweet potatoes, yams, &c., and leaves it to Nature to ripen them. Mr. Schomburgk saw, on the Canucu mountains, at a height of 3,000 ft. above sea-level, bunches of plantains which might have vied with the largest from the island of Puerto-Rico. Different species of vanilla occur along the banks of the rivers; and tobacco is indigenous. The forests supply many kinds of excellent timber, among which are the teak, the greenheart, the mora, and the sewari; many fine cabinet-woods may also be procured in the interior. —The mineral wealth of D. has been as yet but little explored. The Dutch, it is said, made several attempts at mining in the early part of the 18th cent., but with little success. Red and brown iron ores are sometimes met with in the granitic regions which traverse Guayana in a SE direction. Specimens of native silver have been seen on the head-streams of the Rio-Branco; and the precious metals may, it is thought, exist in the Pacaraima mountains from which the streams descend. Almost at the moment of our going to press with this article, the Demerara Royal Gazette mentions, on the authority of the governor of Oronoque in Venezuela, the discovery of valuable gold mines in the district of Upata, near the river Urari, in the prov. of Guayana, and at a distance of only five days' journey from the city of Bolivar, consequently in the immediate vicinity of the colony of British Guayana. The particular spot where the mines are situated is at the foot of an extensive range of mountains not far from the river Cuyuwini, whose lower course intersects British Guayana. Specimens of the gold had been brought to the city of Bolivar by the Indians inhabiting the frontiers between British and Venezuelan Guayana. It was found for the most part imbedded in stones of a pure white colour, and was pronounced to be of the finest quality by the jewellers by whom it had been tested in Bolivar. The Gazette remarks that this information is by no means improbable, as Guayana has been but little explored, the coasts only being cultivated by Europeans; and that ever since the time of Sir Walter Raleigh it has been a prevalent idea that this portion of the American continent is the seat of mines of the precious metals to an inexhaustible extent. Plantations.] The plantations along the D., and in other parts of the united colony, were surveyed, and laid out in portions of 500 acres, by the Dutch West India company, with a conditional grant of as much more behind the former, when two-thirds of it should be cultivated. To this grant all the estates along the D. are entitled. Every plantation has a wharf or landing-place opposite to the dwelling-house; and is surrounded with canals and sluices for the double purpose of draining all superfluous moisture from the land, and of harbouring boats while they are loading and discharging. Each estate is thus completely insulated; and for the convenience of travellers, a bridge is thrown over the canals on each side, which the proprietor is obliged to keep in repair, and to have painted white in order to be discernible in the dark.—The annual average of the eight best cotton estates in the beginning of this cent. was, according to Pinckard, about 60,000 lbs. each. In the cultivation of this plant, one prime Negro was considered as sufficient stock—in the language of planters—for 2 acres of land. The average number of cotton-bushes planted upon an acre is 600; and each bush is calculated to produce 8 oz. of cotton. An intelligent estate-manager reports regarding the cultivation of cotton in this colony: "There is no country under the sun more favourable for growing all sorts of it, from Sea-Island to Upland cotton, and, besides the immense tract of coast from the Courentyne river to the Essequibo, there is a tract of land to the northward of nearly 100 sq. m., having large rivers and creeks, of the finest description in the world for producing cotton, which will produce two crops per annum for an unlimited time. A great deal of this land is perfectly well adapted for producing two crops per annum of cocoa and coffee."-The requisite proportion of stock for an estate cultivated with coffee is 2 Negroes to 3 acres of land. The number of coffee-bushes usually planted is 450 per acre; and each bush is calculated to produce 24 ounces of coffee. -The cultivation of sugar is more expensive, and requires considerably the greatest proportion of live stock, and manual labour, one prime Negro being necessary to every acre of land. The average value of a sugar-plantation is £50 sterling per acre, being 2,000 lbs. of sugar at 4d. per lb. and rum in proportion; about 80 gall, of rum are expected from every hhd, of sugar; and the D. rum has a richness of flavour, which gives it a preference in the American markets. It does not, however, appear that the planters of D. have yet been able to compete successfully with the sugar-planters of the West India islands. Possessing abundance of fertile soil yet unbroken, and readily accessible-an advantage shared with it, to anything like the same extent, by no other English colony excepting the neighbouring island of Trinidad— Trinidad it was £105; in Jamaica it was only £44 15s.; and in the other colonies the general average was under £50. These inequalities arose from the prohibition of inter-colonial migration by slaves,—a measure found requisite to the complete suppression which this colony took the lead both in denouncing the compensation as insufficient, and in demanding of the home-government the means of supplying itself with labour by immigration from Africa or elsewhere. Further, it is to be observed that as sugar raised from a rank virgin-soil by rude labour is naturally of an inferior quality to that of a naturally well-drained soil, and in the culture and manufacture of which a larger share of skill is of the sugar-cane than either Cuba or the best districts of Brazil, and much of the land, moreover, requires to be protected from the sea by costly embankments, polders, and canals, the planters of British Guayana might justly feel even more dread of the apbacked by the common interest of the remaining colonies, always taking a very prominent, if not the most prominent part in opposing the advances of the home-government first towards the establishment of free labour, and afterwards towards free trade. [Globe.] It even appears that under free competition the sugarplanters of Guayana have difficulty in maintaining their ground, not against the Brazilians and Cubans only, but against Barbadoes and Trinidad. Of the sugar-cultivation in Barbadoes the author of Eight Years in British Guiana, from 1840 to 1848 [Lond. 1850]. says: "It is the low rate of wages which enables them to hold out so well, coupled with the high comparative prices which their sugar commands in the market,-the latter advantage being invariably the concomitant of a poor and long-cultivated soil. How their wages are but one-third of ours is not so easily explained. It can only be owing-for no other satisfactory cause has been assigned-to the extremely dense pop. of the island; yet, it is very surprising that the amor patrix should be so strong among the Barbadian Negroes as to keep them at home on 6d. a-day, when, by sailing 500 m., they could get 20d., and all the advantages besides enjoyed by the labourers here. Nevertheless, such seems to be the case, for very few of them have settled permanently here since the year 1838, though many have come. They have, in this cheap labour, an immense advantage, which enables them to make the most of their sterile soil. In regard to manuring, as it is a practice which has long obtained in the islands, they have not to begin, as we have, in this dreadful transition-state, and to drill the people to it under adverse circumstances. The Barbadian planters procure manure from England, in addition to what they collect on the estates in a thousand ways." Attempts have been made to supply the want of labouring hands in Guayana by the importation of foreign labourers. Since 1835, 33,852 souls have found their way into British Guayana, and in 1846 the number of labourers introduced was 11,519. Of this immigration 15-16ths have been Coolies from the East Indies, who appear to have disappointed all the expectations which had been formed of them, being neither so able nor so willing to work for money-wages as the Creoles. "The feeling of the community, including not a few of the planters themselves." says an article in the Royal Gazette of 3d August, 1850, "appears to be decidedly averse to the colony having any further connection, upon the old terms, with these Asiatic labourers. The Coolies have already proved a very dear bargain to the country. which is as badly off now as before the experiment of importing them was commenced in 1845. It is true that in the intervening four years of 1846, 1847, 1848, and 1849, individual proprietors may have derived some temporary advantage from the services of these people. An estate here and an estate there would probably have been abandoned but for a welcome addition of 40 or 50 Coolies to its labour-gang. Still it is questionable whether the condition of the colony as an exporting community has been much improved by the large and costly experiment of 1845. That experiment told, at the earliest, on the sugar crop of 1846. The sugar-cane is a plant which takes some time to grow. The sugarplanter, besides being more than usually industrious and vigilant. must be a man capable of waiting. The nature of sugar-cultivation is such, that labour expended upon the soil in 1845 could not have yielded any return before 1846. Therefore, though the first batches of Coolies were imported here in 1845, the crop of that year was in nowise produced by Coolie labour. Now, to judge of the general value of our Coolie importations, let us take the exports of sugar and coffee during the four years preceding 1846, namely 1842, 1843, 1844, and 1845, and compare them with those of the last four; for this appears to us the only way of testing the question what the community at large, as an exporting country, has gained by Coolie labour. The sugar and coffee erops, it will be seen, of the four years affected by Coolie labour, were: SUGAR. Whereas, during the preceding four years the exports of these crops were: SUGAR. Making a difference in favour of the last four years, ending 1849, as regards sugar, of 6,843 hhds.; but, upon the other hand, in favour of the four first years, ending 1845, as regards coffee, of 5,031,453 lbs. Thus, it will be seen, we are under the Coolie system, after spending vast sums of money upon it, at the best, very much where we were, in an agricultural point of view, after the experiment as before it. We have gained, in point of exports, 6,843 hhds. of sugar, and lost 5,031,453 lbs of coffee. For our own part, we are inclined to suspect, that the coffee lost was worth more than the sugar gained, and that somehow or other, in spite of all our efforts, all our experience, and all our Coolies, we have gone backwards in the world instead of forwards. The inference we draw from these premises is, that it is utterly useless for us to renew Coolie immigration upon the old conditions. But we have lost something more than a few millions of coffee, in return for a few thousand hhds, of sugar. By a return from the financial accountant, called for by a petition very numerously signed, and published in our number of the 27th ult., it turns out that, exclusive of 'certain general expenses' already incurred, and the return passages which have yet to be met, we have actually expended since 1845 the immense sum of 947,816 dols. on Coolie immigration; namely, 808,068 dols. on bounties paid here, and 139,747 dols. spent in India. In other words, we have spentwith what result has been seen-about £200,000 sterling, or exactly the same sum which we propose to spend again by the aid of the parliamentary loan of £250,000-£50,000 of which is to go to the railway company-on the introduction of Coolie labourers. The vast difference, however, between our former and our present expenditure will be this, -that whereas formerly we managed to get rid of the £200,000 without permanently saddling the colony with debt, we shall now have to mortgage the revenues of the colony for a series of years before we can obtain a similar Coolie immigration fund. Thus much for Coolies. As regards the other two classes of foreign labourers on whom the colony is dependent, Africans and Madeirans, we have to report that on the 27th ult. 268 Africans arrived here from Sierra Leone by the Glentanner. They are most of them young people, and have proved a very acceptable addition to our permanent population; for we look upon the Coolies as constituting nothing more than a floating population. And, as regards Madeirans, we have to report that, with the view of encouraging immigration from Madeira, the court of policy has raised the bounty on each Madeiran introduced here from 20 dols. to 30 dols. These people are a very industrious race; the only objections to them as agricultural labourers being that they are apt to be very sickly on their first arrival, and that as soon as they have managed to save a little money—which, with their frugality, they are not long in doing— they quit the field for other more congenial employments. On the other hand, most of them make the colony their home; and they are domestic and orderly in their habits. A measure was recently brought before the legislature for allowing proprietors to bring here, at their own cost, a new and fourth class of immigrants—we mean Chinese. We understand that one gentleman connected with estates has written out for 50 of these people." The antagonism between the recently emancipated Negro labourer and the white capitalist has been the great impediment in the experiment of free labour in Guayana. The sugar-planters and their agents supposing it to be their interest to keep the emancipated Negroes and the Creole labourers, in a condition as nearly approaching to serfdom as possible, have alienated them moment from the scheme,—regarded it as impolitic and unjust. The Creole labourers, driven from estates by the introduction of the Coolies, became purchasers of land and growers of provisions; and now they have become quite a distinct class, no less than 50,000 of them being settled on their own lands, in villages erected, peopled, and managed by themselves. In Berbice alone, the late emancipated labourers have purchased, paid for, and settled above 10,000 acres of land; that is at the rate of 2 acres for every Creole family, a quantity by no means sufficient fully to employ them, or to enable them to advance in civilization and their own. They were earning more money than it cost them to live, and employing it in buying better clothes, building better houses, making better roads, paying their own ministers and schoolmasters, purchasing books, building schools and chapels, and giving money to the missionaries to send home to assist the societies here in sending knowledge to less enlightened lands. No people in the world were so rapidly improving, and the knowledge of their improvement would have spread to over-populated II. afford such excellent pasture that there is every reason to hope these colonies will yet be made to prove an abundant source of wealth to the grazier, and an unfailing field of supply to the West India islands. Population, &c.] The pop. of D. consists of 4 classes, Whites, Creoles, Negroes, and Coolies. The number of Negroes belonging to the united colony amounted, in 1798, to 55,000; in 1806, to upwards of 80,000; in 1826, to 71,382; in 1829, to 78,734; in 1834, to 74,992; and in 1847, to 121,678. Previous to the abolition of the slave-trade, in 1807, the importation of slaves into this settlement, and that of Berbice, was immense, -more perhaps than the numbers im ported into all the West India islands put together. The total pop. on the 31st Dec., 1844, amounted to 103,950, which shows an increase from natural causes during that year of 1,226. The number of emancipated peasantry now living in their own dwellings in Guayana is estimated at 50,000. The number of emancipated Negroes now become freeholders, amounts altogether to about 10,000. As to the character of the colonial pop., it is much the same with that in the West Indies, or in the southern United States. Commerce.] The total produce of the united colony of D. and Essequibo, for 1816, was returned at, sugar, 44,030,381 lbs.; rum, 1,956,395 gallons; coffee, 11,683,647 lbs.; cotton, 3,818,512 lbs. In 1823, this country exported to Great Britain only, 607,807 cwt. of sugar, 54,147 cwt. of coffee, and 941,194 gallons of rum. In 1836, the exports amounted in value to £1,595,137. The exports into the United Kingdom in 1845, amounted to £992,678; whilst those from Berbice amounted to £234,365. The imports from Great Britain into D. and Essequibo, in the same year, were £410,911; into Berbice, £62,573. The exports of produce from Georgetown for the year ending 30th Nov., 1849, were: -Sugar, 28,399 hhds., 3,433\(\frac{1}{2}\) tierces, 12,231 barrels; rum, 12,570 puns., 3,040 hhds., 1,260 barrels; molasses, 13,945 puns., 267 hhds., 131 barrels; coffee, 25 tierces, 387 barrels, 138 bags; timber, 1,976 logs; and hides, 2,402. The exports of sugar from D. and Essequibo, from the 1st of January to the 30th of November, for the last five years, is returned as follows:- . 28,103 hhds. The exports of the four principal articles of produce from all British Guayana for the last ten years, from the 1st of January to the 31st of December, was as follows:- Molasses. Rum. Coffee. Sugar. Year. Puns. Puns. Dutch lbs. Hhds. The following is a return of the number of vessels, and the amount of tonnage, which entered the port of Demerara, in the undermentioned years: Tonnage. Vessels. Further and later details relative to the commerce and trade of these counties will be found in the article GUAYANA. Revenue.] The revenue and expenditure of D. and Essequibo were as follows in the years noted: Expenditure. Revenue. Towns.] The capital of D. is Georgetown, formerly called Stabroeck, situated 1 m. from the mouth of the D. river, on the E or r. bank, in N lat. 6° 49′ 20", W long. 58° 11′ 30". It is composed of two long rows of houses built very distant from each other, with a wide green in the middle by way of street. It is more than 1 m. long. The form of the town is the most inconvenient that could have been contrived, as it throws most of the houses far away from the river, and deprives them of the great advantages for trade which they might otherwise have had by erecting the houses parallel with its course. To remedy this defect, canals and ditches have been cut at the backs of the houses, which are perhaps the worst possible neighbours the inhabitants could have near them, for, being filled with mud and all the filthy drainings of the town, and only partially emptied by the reflux of the tide, they become highly offensive, and tend to generate disease. The pop. is about 30,000.—On the W bank of the Mahaica river is a thriving v. of the same name, with a pop. of about 1,200. The river, which is 70 yds. wide, and 40 ft. deep, at the v. is crossed by a bridge; and a railway from Georgetown to this place is in course of construction.—Craig's village, on the r. bank of the D. river, is a thriving settlement, now containing a pop. of 1,500. There are villages of magnitude at Den Amstel, and Vries-en-Hoop; and the islands of Leguan and Wakenhaam, at the mouth of the D., are covered with hamlets, villages, and free settlements.—A penal settlement has been formed on the I. bank of the Essequibo, at the point where the united waters of the Mazaruni and the Cayuni pour themselves into it.—The two united counties are divided into 11 parishes.—The NW portion of the country lying between the Pomarun and the Orinoco does not yet appear to have received any European settlers. Historical Notice.] D., originally settled by the Dutch, is of later growth than Berbice, which was made a colony as early as 1626. But the progress of D. has been much more rapid than that of its sister-settlement, especially since its capture, and subsequent cession to Great Britain. Its capture by the British took place in 1796, when a number of adventurers, both from the islands and Great Britain, flocked to it. Along with Essequibo, it was restored to the Dutch in 1801, after several millions of British money had been expended, both by British planters and merchants, in improvements and purchases. This cession was a very serious loss, and was one of those articles of the treaty of Amiens which occasioned great regret and discontent. They were, however, retaken in 1803, and secured to Britain at the peace of 1814.—The settlement of Essequibo, immediately adjoining D. to the NW, and lying along the banks of the Essequibo, is a colony of much later date than D., its settlement being begun in 1742, since which it has advanced with rapid strides, and is now of equal value and importance with D., to which it is united. In 1831, the two counties of D. and Essequibo were united with that of Berbice under one government, called Bri- TISH GUAYANA; and the reader is referred to that article for further details, and for a combined view of the whole settlement.

DEMETRIO (Santo), a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Ultra, district and 11 m. SE of Aquila. Pop. 2,000. It contains 3 churches, and has an annual fair. The surrounding district produces large quantities of almonds and saffron.—Also a town in the prov. of Calabria-Citra, in the district and 18 m. W of Rossano, and 21 m. NNE of Cosenza. Pop., chiefly Albanians, 1,840. It contains a Catholic and a Greek church. A col- DEM lege was founded here in 1791 by Ferdinand IV., for the benefit of the Albanian youth.
DEMETRIUS (CAPE SAINT), a promontory of Turkey in Europe, on the E coast of the prov. of Thessaly, in N lat. 39° 18'. A village of the same name stands upon it.
DEMIANKA, a river of Russia in Asia, in the Irtish at Demiansk.
DEMIANOVO, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Pskov, 35 m. ENE of Kholm.
DEMIANSK, a town of Russia in Asia, in the gov. and 95 m. NNE of Tobolsk, at the confluence of the Demianka with the Irtish. It contains about 100 houses, inhabited chiefly by Russians.
DEMIGHRAD, a village of Upper Egypt, in the prov. of Thebes, 20 m. NNW of Esneh, on the 1. bank of the Nile.
DEMIGNY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Chagny. Pop. 1,659.
DEMIRDJILER, a small town of Turkey in Europe, in Rumelia, in the sanjak of Sophia, 16 m. N of Philippopolis.

DEMIRGHOI, a district in the Western Caucasus, lying along the upper course of the Shaukwassa river, which has its source in the Snowy mountains, near Mount Shagalish, and flows into the Kuban nearly opposite Plastimiwurskoi. The neighbourhood of the upper part of this river, and the high mountain-ridge that extends towards Ghelendjik and Pshad, is the chosen habitat of perhaps the fiercest of all the Caucasian races.

DEMIRHISSAR, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Macedonia, cap. of a sanjak, 15 m. NNW of Seres. Pop. 8,000. It is built at the foot of a lofty mountain, on the summit of which is the castle from which it takes its name; and is surrounded by walls flanked with towers. A small stream—an affluent of the Karasu—runs through its centre, and affords in its passage propelling power to several mills. This town occupies the site of the ancient Heraclius.
DEMIRJI-CHAI, a river of Asia Minor, in Anatolia—the ancient Hyllus—which descends from the S side of the Demirji-Dagh, runs SSW, and falls into the Kodus.

DEMIRJI-DAGH, a mountain-range of Asia Minor, in the NW of Anatolia, running along the S side of the basin of the Simaul-su, from Pergammon on the W to the lofty Ak-Dagh or Shapkan-Dagh on the E. This range is composed of schistose rocks, with saccharine marble, tertiary sandstone and limestone, granite, trachyte, and basalt. About 7 m. to the E of Singarli, at the N foot of the D. chain, are hot springs of a very high temp.—On the N side of the river is the town of Demirji-Khoi, in a rich wellcultivated oval plain, stretching about 14 m. from N to S, and from 3 to 5 m. in breadth. DEMIRKAPI [i. e. 'the Iron gate'], a narrow valley through which the Danube flows, a few m. below Orsova, extending from Nova Moldava in the Banat, to Chernitz in Wallachia. It is bounded on the N by the Banat range of the Carpathians, and on the S by a lateral range of the Balkan system. The river here rushes with great rapidity over a vast plateau of rock filling up the whole channel. A canal has been projected along the Servian shore at this point. See DANUBE.
DEMIRKAPU, a small town of Turkey in Europe, in Macedonia, in the sanjak and 85 m. SSW of Ghiustendil, on the r. bank of the Bagna, an affluent of the Vardar.
DEMITI, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, flowing into the Rio-Negro, on the l. bank, near São-José.

DEMJYOKH, a town of Ladakh, on the Indus, 1 where the Sikh and Chinese frontiers meet. The alt. of this place must approach 12,000 ft., and the therm. was observed here by Lieut. Strachey for two or three days in October as low as 6° and 7° F.

DEMMIN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Pomerania, cap. of a circle of the same name, in the regency and 75 m. WNW of Stettin, in an advantageous situation on the Peene, at the confluence of the Trebel and Tollensee. Pop. 6,140. It contains a church, an hospital, a school, &c.; and has extensive manufactories of cloth, linen, and hosiery, several tanneries and bleachfields, important distilleries of gin, breweries, &c. The trade, which is considerable, consists chiefly in grain, wood, and glass-ware. Fairs are held 4 times a-year. This town is of great antiquity, and is noted for the number of sieges which it has sustained. Its fortifications were destroyed in 1759. In 1807 several engagements took place here between the French and Russians. -The circle of D. contains 3 towns, 166 villages, and 27,427 inhabitants.
DEMNIA, or DEMNA, a village of Galicia, in the gov. of Lemberg, and circle of Itry. Freestone is extensively quarried in the environs.
DEMONE (VAL-DI-), an old administrative division of Sicily, embracing the NE part of the island, and of which the cap. was Messina, now comprised in the districts of Messina, Catania, and Palermo.

DEMONTE, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandamento, in the prov. and 15 m. SW of Coni, on the 1. bank of the Stura. Pop. 6,009. It is defended by a fort; and contains 3 churches—one of them a Greek church—a college, and an hospital. In 1774 it was taken by the united forces of France and Spain, and its fortifications demolished. They have since been repaired.

DEMOPOLIS, a village of Marengo co., in the state of Alabama, U. S., 64 m. SSW of Tuscaloosa, on the E bank of Tombigbee river, near the confluence of Black Warrior river. It consisted in 1840 of about 150 dwellings.
DEMORESTVILLE, a village of Upper Canada, in the township of Sophiasburg, on the bay of Quinta, in Prince Edward district. Pop. 400.
DEMOTICOS. See DIMOTEKA.

DEMSUS, or Demsos, a village of Transylvania, in the co. of Hunyad. It occupies the site of the Ulpia-Trajana or Zarmiza-Gethusa of the ancients, and contains a Roman temple, which is now used as a church.
DEMU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gers, cant. of Eauze. Pop. 1,012.
DEMZENAN, a town of Persia, in the prov. and about 60 m. ENE of Kirman, in a mountainous locality. It is surrounded by mines of iron, copper, and sal-ammoniac.
DENABUDDELECHE, a town of Abyssinia, in the united prov. of Shoa and Efat, 60 m. E of Ankober, near the l. bank of the Hawash.
DENABY, or Dannaby, a township in the p. of Maxborough, W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 1,080 acres. Pop. 167.
DENAIKENCOTTA, or DANAIKENCOTA, a town of Hindostan, in the presidency of Madras, prov. and 30 m. NNE of Coimbatur, on the l. bank of the Bovany.

DENAIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Bouchain, 18 m. E of Douai, near the l. bank of the Scheldt. Pop. in 1841, 5,144. It is noted for its abbey or chapter of canonesses founded in 784. Beet-root sugar forms the chief article of local manufacture. Coal and iron are extensively wrought in the environs. The important engage- | hill to the S of Ruthin, and flowing NW through the DEN ment between the Dutch and Imperialists, in 1712which led to the peace of Utrecht-took place here.
DENARDISTON, or DENSTON, a parish of Suffolk. Area 1,230 acres. Pop. 339.
DENAT, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Tarn, cant. of Réalmont, 9 m. SSE of Alby. Pop. 880.
DENAZE'-GRAND-BOIS, a village of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Craon, 11 m. WNW of Chateau-Gontier. Pop. 536. Slate is quarried in the environs.

DENBIGH, a parish, borough, and market-town in the co. of Denbigh, 201 m. W by N of London, and 5 m. S of St. Asaph, to the W of the river Clwyd, and on the banks of the Ilshod, one of its tributaries. The town is situated on a steep acclivity, overhung by a rock, and crowned by the ruins of its noble castle, whence there is an extensive prospect of the vale of Clwyd, with Ruthin in the distance on the one hand, St. Asaph's on the other, and the blue tops of the distant mountains environing the whole. The town consists principally of a spacious market-place, and several elegant residences, constituting one long handsome avenue or street, whence other smaller ones diverge. Tanners, glovers, and shoemakers, who produce goods which are chiefly sent to London for exportation, are the principal tradesmen; but D. is more a place of genteel retirement than of much trade. Pop. in 1801, 2,391; in 1831, 3,786; in 1841, 5,238, of whom only 3,405 were in the p. The revenue of the borough, in 1832, was £242 3s. 8d.; in 1846, it was £123. Under the reform act it sends one member to parliament, in conjunction with Ruthin, Holt, and Wrexham, as contributories. The number of electors registered in 1847 was 847, out of a joint pop. of 15,388. The boundaries of the parl, and municipal borough are co-extensive. Under the municipal act, the borough is governed by 4 aldermen and 12 councillors. History.] D. was anciently of great military importance. David, brother of the last Llewellyn, made it his rendezvous when he summoned the Welsh chieftains to form a coalition against the English. After his defeat the lordship was granted by Edward I. to Henry Lacy, earl of Lincoln, who built the castle and walled the town. Edward granted it to the Mortimer family; and, again reverting to the Crown, Elizabeth bestowed it on her favourite, Dudley, earl of Leicester. In 1646, the castle was valiantly defended by William Salisbury, but was finally surrendered to the parliamentary army. At the Restoration, it was totally demolished. The ruins of this venerable structure are very extensive. The walls are of a very singular construction, having been grouted or formed of two thick walls filled up between with stones and hot cement, which bound the whole together in one mass of such strength as in the early ages must have rendered the castle impregnable.—The triennial meeting of the Welsh bards, called the Eistedfod, is occasionally held in this town.

DENBIGHSHIRE, one of the six divisions of N. Wales; bounded on the N by the Irish sea; on the E by the cos. of Flint, Chester, and Salop; on the W by Carnarvon; on the S by Montgomery and Merioneth, into the latter of which a narrow stripe runs towards the SE. From Merionethshire this co. is separated by the Bereryn mountains; from Flintshire on the E by the river Dee; from Carnarvonshire by the river Conwy. It is irregular in form: its greatest length is 48 m.; greatest breadth, about 30 m. From Llanrwst on the Conwy, to Holt on the Dee, it is 36 m. in length; from St. Asaph to Yobytty-Eran, 19 m. Its narrowest breadth, over the vale of Clwyd, is 9 m. Its circumf. is 170 m., and its superficial area has been calculated by some at 387,600 acres; by others at 400,000. In the report of county rates it is estimated at 405,120 acres, and in the highway returns at 633 sq. m. Rivers, &c.] The principal river is the Clwyd, one of the sources of which issues from the bottom of a beautiful vale of Clwyd, and on the skirts of the co., flows past St. Asaph in Flintshire, to the Irish sea .- The Dee crosses this co. in a NE direction from Merionethshire into Cheshire; the Conwy separates it from Carnarvon.—The Elwy, a tributary of the Clwyd, rises in the SW part of the co., and running N and NW, falls into the Clywd near St. Asaph.— The Alen, a tributary of the Dee, rises near Llandegla; flows N through Flintshire; turns suddenly through Hopedale to Gresford; and re-entering Denbighshire, joins the D. a little below Holt.—The Ceiriog, a tributary of the Dee, rises on the W side of the co., and, forming a part of the boundary between England and Wales, falls into the D. near Chirk.—The Clwyd, the Conwy, and the Dee, are the chief channels through which this co. is drained; but it has no river-navigation, neither has it any port or tolerable haven, although in some measure a maritime co.-The Ellesmere canal, which unites the Severn, the Dee, and the Mersey, has a navigable feeder from the Dee, whence it runs towards Ruabon, and at Llantisilio crosses the Dee by the celebrated aqueduct of Pont-y-Sysselltan, which is supported by in the elevation and quality of its surface and soil. Towards the N it has the appearance of an extensive plain. The celebrated vales of Clwyd, Llangollen, Conwy, and Valle-Crucis, are all very extensive and very beautiful. The E part of the co. is enclosed by a range of rugged hills commencing near St. Asaph's. The loftiest of these, Moel-Fammau, is elevated 1,845 ft. above the level of the sea. A second range encloses the co. on the SW, commencing at the sea, and extending as far as the Merionethshire hills to the S of Moelwynne-Mawr. The loftiest summit of this range is Modwl-Erthin, 1,660 ft. These western heathy hills are interspersed with narrow vales and small lakes. In some districts of the S, SE, and N. the soil consists of fine pasture and meadow land, on which cheese equal to the celebrated Cheshire is produced. Some of the finest wheat in England is grown in the D. vales. Rye and barley are cultivated on all the hills, and oats on the uplands. Agriculture and sheep-husbandry are fostered, and have been much improved in this co. by two societies instituted for their improvement. Sheep and goats, in immense numbers, are reared on the heaths and mountains, and wool is here manufactured into flannels, coarse cloths, and stockings. The climate is esteemed salutary; but, in districts exposed to the northern ocean blasts, is ungenial and even rigorous and severe. Black cattle are reared on the hilly pastures. Immense quantities of peat almost like coal, are procured in the mountainous districts. - The minerals are not varied; but rich beds of lead, iron, and coal have been found. Those parts of Bromfield and Yale bordering on Flintshire, comprise a part of the mineral tract, which passes in a N direction through that co. to the estuary of the Dee. On the S it enters below Mold; extends through the parishes of Llanferres, Llanarmon, and Llandegla, whence it branches off to Minera, and terminates at the Glesseg rocks in the vicinity of Llangollen. Iron ore, lime, and valuable coal, are dug out of the Ruabon hills-where there are iron-forges and a cannon-foundry-and in the adjacent and eastern parts of the co. At Bromba, in the vicinity of Wrexham, iron ore of a peculiarly excellent quality is obtained; and near Pont-y- Sysselltan, a sulphur-mine containing iron pyrites in masses was some years ago discovered. Lead ore of マー・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア the purest kind is raised in the vale of Llanrwst; slate of excellent and durable quality-especially at Chirk - with limestone, grindstones, and freestone for building, are found in various other quarters.-The great parliamentary Holyhead mail-road, between Ireland and the metropolis, enters this co. at Chirk, and running past Llangollen, enters Merionethshire; whence it re-enters this co., running along the narrow stripe which penetrates into Merioneth, towards Llanrwst; near which it crosses the Conwy, and finally leaves the co. By these two modes of conveyance the trade of D. has been greatly increased, as it is now likely still farther to be, by the introduction of railways. The mail-roads are good, but many of the cross roads are very bad. The other principal roads of the co. are those which unite the towns of D., Wrexham, Ruthin, and Llangollen with each other, and with the surrounding cos. The highway returns for 1839 give 1,068 m. as the length of highways in this co. Divisions, &c.] This co. is divided into the 6 cantrefs or hundreds of Pop. in 1831. Pop. in 1841. Bromfield, There are 64 parishes in this co., which is partly in the dio. of St. Asaph, and partly in that of Bangor, and in the prov. of Canterbury. In 1831 there were 141 daily schools, attended by 5,430 children; and 211 Sunday schools, attended by 20,773. Of these. 8 daily schools, with 302 children, and 154 Sunday schools, with 14,411 children, belonged to Dissenters. —The chief towns are the boroughs of Denbigh and Ruthin, and the market-towns of Wrexham, Llanrwst, Abergelly, Ruabon, Llangollen, and Chirk.- The co. returns 2 members to parliament, who are polled at Denbigh, Wrexham, Llanrwst, Llangollen. and Ruthin; the principal place of election being Denbigh. The number of electors registered for 1837 was 3,689; in 1846, 3,939. As the borough of D., with its contributories, returns 1, there are in all 3 members for the co. The county-court is held every fourth Wednesday alternately at D. and at Wrexham. Pop. in 1801, 60,352, of whom 9,960 were employed in trade, &c., and 21,104 in agriculture; in 1821, 76,511; in 1831, 82,800, of whom 8,135 families were employed in agriculture, 4,178 in trade, &c., and 4,537 otherwise employed; in 1841, 88,866.— Amount of poor rates on an average of 3 years, from 1748 to 1750, £1,139, all expended on poor; for 1803, £24,480, expenditure on poor £19,954; for 1821, £43,643, expenditure on poor £36,362; for 1839, £38,500, expenditure on poor £31,100; for 1846, £44,497. History and Antiquities.] This co., denominated in Welsh Sir.

DINBECH, was included at the time of the Roman invasion in that part of Cambria occupied by the Ordovices. Under the Romans, it made a part of Venedotia, one of the minor partitions of the imperial division of the island, then denominated Britannia Secunda. During the almost continual dissensions between the Mercians and the Welsh, D. was often the arena and the object of their contests. One vestige remains of their mode of warfare, in an immense ditch and rampart constructed by Offa, a Mercian king. and named Offa's dyke. This singular work-which in many places is almost entire-extends from the river Wye along the cos. of Hereford, Radnor, and Montgomery; enters D. near Chirk castle; quits it above Wrexham; and ends at Mold in Flint. Nearly parallel to this celebrated trench, on the English side, is a similar fortification, which extended from Maesbury near Oswestry, to the Dee at Basingwerk. The Norman period commenced with the subjugation of this co. and its partition amongst military adventurers, -a system pursued after the Conquest, and especially under Henry III. But it was left to Edward L to consummate the subjugation of the entire prov. to the English yeke.
DENBIGH (CAPE), a headland of Russian America, on the W coast, at the head of Norton sound, in N lat. 64° 40', W long. 162°.
DENBURY, a parish of Devon, 5 m. SW of Ashburton. Area 1,270 acres. Pop. 470.

DENBY, a parish of Derbyshire, 7 m. NNE of Derby, E of the Derwent, and on the Leeds and Derby railway. Area 2,380 acres. Pop. 1,338.— Also a township in the p. of Penistone, W. R. of Yorkshire, 8 m. W of Barnesley, at the source of the Dearne. Area 2,870 acres. Pop. 1,690.
DENCHWORTH, a parish of Berks, 21 m. NW of Wantage, on the Great Western railway. Area 1,060 acres. Pop. 246.

DENDER, or DENDRE, a river of Belgium, formed by the union of two streams at Ath, in the prov. of Hainault, one of which rises 4 m. NW of Leuze, and the other to the E of Herchies; running NNE, it passes Lessines; enters the prov. of E. Flanders; passes Grammont, Ninove, and Alost; and discharges itself into the Schelde, on the r. bank, at Dendermonde, after a course of 63 m., of which 48 have been rendered navigable by means of locks. Coals form the chief article of transit on this river.

DENDER, a river of Africa, which takes its rise in Abyssinia, in the prov. of Dembea, to the NW of the source of the Blue Nile; runs NW along the confines of Abyssinia to the junction of the Golongo; thence enters the kingdom of Sennaar in Nubia; and after a total course of 300 m., falls into the Bahr-el- Azrek or Blue Nile, 30 m. SSE of the confluence of the Rahad, and 70 m. N of Sennaar. It is of great breadth and depth, and according to Brune, rivals in its volume, when inundated, that of the Nile. Its banks are fertile, and are adorned with trees. The soil is remarkable for the redness of its colour.

DE'NDERAH, a village of Upper Egypt, in the prov. and 33 m. N of Thebes, on the l. bank of the Nile, 4 m. W of Gheneh. It is embosomed in a grove of doum palms; but with the exception of a Coptic church, contains nothing worthy of note. In its vicinity, however, about 2 m. from the banks of the Nile towards the W, in N lat. 30° 21', are the celebrated ruins of the ancient Tentyris. Of these magnificent remains, probably of Ptolemaic antiquity, the most remarkable is a temple 250 ft. in length, and 108 ft. in breadth, in perfect preservation, with two porticos, one consisting of 24 Isis-headed columns, 43 ft. in height, and 23 ft. in circumf., covered to the summit with paintings and hieroglyphical sculpture, "strange mythological fancies," human forms with the heads of rams, cows, hawks, vultures, crocodiles, and crowned with the mitres of priesthood or royalty. The door, which is of great size, and the inner walls and ceilings, are all similarly adorned; and form fine specimens of the high degree of perfection in art which had been attained by the ancient Egyptians. On the ceiling of one of the upper halls was what has been termed a representation of the Zodiac, encircled by two female figures. It was cut out, and removed to Paris in 1821.—The pasha has established works at D. for extracting saltpetre from the ruins. Mr. Bowring was informed that after the nitre has been extracted from these ruins, they become saturated again after a few years, and may be passed through the pans, depositing almost as much nitre as before.

DENDERMONDE, or TERMONDE, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of Belgium, in the prov. of E. Flanders.—The arrond. comprises 10 cant.—The town is situated at the confluence of the Dender with the Schelde, 16 m. E of Ghent, on the railway between Ghent and Malines. Pop. 8,548. It is strongly fortified, and has a citadel, which can be isolated at pleasure by the adjacent rivers. Its general char- DEN acter is that of "a gloomy, silent, unattractive town." [Tennant.] It contains a college, a drawing academy, and a public library; has manufactories of woollen fabrics, tulle, printed calicoes, cordage, leather, and soap; and also possesses oil-mills, potteries, and several salt-refineries. Its commerce, consisting in grain, hemp, lint, oil, brandy, &c., is extremely active.—This town advances pretensions to have been founded in the 8th cent.; little, however, is known respecting its history previous to 1061. Its citadel was built in 1584, by the Duke of Parma. In 1667 it was besieged ineffectually by Louis XIV., with a force of 50,000 men. In 1745 it fell into the hands of the French. In 1784 it was dismantled by the emperor Joseph II.; but the fortifications were restored a few years since. It suffered severely from an inundation in 1825.

DENDOUD-TIALI, a lake of Senegambia, in the territory of Bondu, which discharges itself by the Nerico into the Gambia on the S, and during inundations forms another outlet on the N, which flows into the Senegal. It is navigable for canoes. It derives its name from a village on its banks.
DENE'E, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. and 12 m. S of Namur. Pop. 458. It has some iron-forges, and extensive quarries of marble and freestone.
DENE'EL-A-NOUE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Chalonnes, 9 m. SSW of Angers. Pop. 1,564.
DENEKAMP, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Overyssel, 6 m. SE of Ootmarsum, and near the r. bank of the Dinkel.
DENEUVRE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, cant. of Bacaret, 16 m. SE of Luneville. Pop. 822. It was formerly a place of considerable strength.
DENFORD, a parish of Northamptonshire, 12 m. S of Thrapston, on the Nen. Area 1,940 acres. Pop. 329.
DENGIE, or DENGEY, a parish of Essex, 31 m. SW of Bradwell. Area 1,750 acres. Pop. 219.
DENGIE MARSH. See NEW ROMNEY.

DENHAM, a parish of Buckinghamshire, 2 m. NNW of Uxbridge, on the E bank of the Misbourne stream. Area 4,850 acres. Pop. 1,264.—Also a p. of Suffolk, 61 m. SW of Bury-St.-Edmond's. Area 1,990 acres. Pop. 182.—Also a p. of the same co., 2½ m. NE of Eye—within the boundaries of which borough it is now included—and near the London and Norwich railway. Area 2,300 acres. Pop. 313.
DENHAM, a village of Holland, in the prov. of Overyssel, 16 m. ENE of Zwolle.
DENHOLM, a village in the p. of Cavers, Roxburghshire, beautifully situated on the r. bank of the Teviot, - which is here crossed by a suspensionbridge,—5 m. from Jedburgh. Pop. 696.

DENIA, a town of Spain, in Valencia, cap. of a judicial partido of the same name, in the prov. and 50 m. NE of Alicante, situated on the N side of Monte Mongo, near the confluence of the Rio-Vergez with the Mediterranean. Pop. 2,980. It is enclosed by walls, and possesses a double port, defended at the entrance, which is difficult of access, by a strong and lofty tower. Excepting the parish-church, 2 convents, the residence of the military governor, and some charitable institutions, it possesses no buildings worthy of note. Woollen fabrics and linen are the chief articles of manufacture. Its trade consists in raisins, almonds, and other local productions.—This town—the ancient Hemeroscopium—was named by Pliny Dianium, from a temple dedicated to Diana, which occupied the summit of the hill on which the citadel now stands.

DENICE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhone, cant. of Villefranche. Pop. 1,131.-Also a village of Piedmont, in the division of Alexandria, prov. and 8 m. WSW of Acqui, near the l. bank of the E branch of the Bormida.
DENINO. See DUNINO.

DENIS (SAINT), an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine. The arrond., comprising an area of 20,211 hectares, contains 4 cant., viz.: St. Denis, Courbevoie, Neuilly, and Pantin. Pop. in 1846, 183,930. The cant. comprises 11 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 19,666; in 1841, 31,860.—The town is situated 6 m. N of Paris, in a fertile plain on the Crou, near the r. bank of the Seine, and of the canal of the same name, which connects the canal of Ourcq with the Seine. Pop. in 1789, 5,330; in 1821, 8,236; in 1836, 9,332; and in 1841, 8,670. It is well-built; and has handsome barracks, an asylum for the houseless poor, a small theatre, a public library, a printing establishment, &c., but is chiefly noted for its church, a fine specimen of Gothic architecture, rebuilt by St. Louis in 1231. It was originally founded in the 12th or 13th cent., by Dagobert in connection with the celebrated Benedictine abbey of St. D., and formed the ordinary place of sepulture of the royal family of France, and of other illustrious personages. By decree of the convention in 1793, the royal tombs were destroyed, and the valuable relics and other treasures of the abbey, including the crown and sword of Charlemagne, the sword of Joan of Arc, the great cross, &c., carried away. The buildings of the abbey are now used as an establishment for the education of 500 daughters of the members of the Legion of Honour. The principal manufactures of St. D. consist in dyed and printed fabrics, chemical substances, and articles in caoutchouc. It has also several bleacheries, cotton spinning-mills, tanneries, corn-mills, &c.; and an active trade in flour, wine, vinegar, wood, and wool. Fairs are held four times a-year. The origin of one of these—distinguished by the name of Landit—is dated as far back as the 7th cent. It is one of the most important sheep fairs in France, its average sale of sheep amounting annually to 100,000. St. D. was originally a small village, named Catolacum. A chapel, which was erected here in the 3d cent., over the tomb of St. Denis the martyr, became the nucleus of the subsequent church. In the reigns of Charles VI. and Charles the town was taken by the English.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Aude, cant. of Saissac, on the Lignon. Pop. 710. It has several iron-forges and paper-mills.-Also a commune and port in the dep. of the Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Saint-Pierre-d'Oleron, in the island of Oleron. Pop. in 1846, 1,200. It has an annual fair. Also a village in the dep. of the Indre-et-Loire, cant. and 1½ m. SE of Amboise, and 15 m. E of Tours. Pop. 837. In the environs are considerable blastfurnaces and forges.—Also a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 5 m. NE of Mons, on the l. bank of a small river. Pop. 680. It has an ancient abbey, now used as a cotton spinning-mill.

DENIS (SAINT), a town of the island of Bourbon, cap. of the colony and of the arrond. du Vent, pleasantly situated at the foot of a mountain, on the N coast of the island, in N lat. 20° 51', and E long. 55° 30'. Pop. 12,000. The houses are built of wood, but are regular and handsome in their structure. The streets are straight, and some of them are adorned with rows of trees. Those nearest the sea contain the government offices and some of the principal commercial stores. The church, the college, the bank, and the barracks are the only other buildings worthy of note. At the extremity of the town is an extensive botanic garden, in which the tea-plant, vanilla, and numerous other valuable pro- DEN ductions are cultivated. St. D. has no port; but the roadstead - which is the best the island possesses - is accessible at all times to vessels of the largest size. It is however destitute of shelter, and consequently exposed to the heavy winds which prevail during eight months of the year. It is defended by a redoubt and batteries; but the difficulty of effecting a landing except by means of boats suited to the shore constitutes its chief defence.-Also an island of the Indian ocean, in the Seychelle archipelago, in S lat. 3° 48', E long. 55° 38'.
DENIS-D'ANJOU (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Bierné, 15 m. E of Château-Gontier. Pop. 2,696. It is noted for its white wines.

DENIS - DE - BRIOUZE (SAINT), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Briouze. and com. of Saint-André-de-Briouze, 17 m. W of Argentan, on the r. bank of the Rouvre, and on the road from Paris to Rennes. Pop. 500. It has extensive manufactories of common earthen-ware. Iron of the finest quality is wrought in the environs.
DENIS-DE-GASTINES (SAINT), a commune and town of France, in the dep. and 13 m. WNW of Mayenne, and cant. of Ernée. Pop. in 1841, 3,448.
DENIS-LA-CHEVASSE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of Le Poirésur-Bourbon. Pop. 1,256.
DENIS-LE-CHOSSON (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ain, cant. of Ambérieux, 27 m. NW of Belley, on the l. bank of the Albarine. Pop. 1,032.
DENIS-LE-GAST (SAINT), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Gavray, 10 m. SSE of Coutances. Pop. 1,859. It has several paper-mills.
DENIS-DE-LA-GADRIE (SAINT), or SAINT
DENIS MAISONCELLES, a village of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Reny-Bocage, 11 m. N of Vire. Pop. 1,691.
DENIS-DE-L'HOTEL (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Loiret, cant. of Château-Neufsur-Loire, 12 m. E of Orleans, on the l. bank of the Loire. Pop. 1,162. It has some trade in wine.
